Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-92-052
Dear Mr. Moore:
Thj.s letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding your duties and respons ibi L it ies under the conflict-of-
inierest -piovisions of the political 
Reform Act (the t'Actt').1
OU ESTI ON S
l. 
what criteria or evidence should a public official 
use to

have a financial effect on that official's 
ownership interest in
real property?

developable portion of the proiect site be counted as part of the
Cevelopment project for purposes of applying Regulation 187o2'3?
3. If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that decisions
regarding the proposed subdivision wilI have a material' f inanci-aI

are ic the Government Code unless otherwise indicated'

regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations. Section

CONCLUSTONS
1. A public official 
i.s required to make a reasonable good
faith effort to determi,ne the financial effects of a governrnental
decision on his econonic interests.
2. Regul,ation LA702.3 does not take into consideration the
contour of the land. ft only sets forth standards for deterrnining
materiality in terns of distance to the boundaries of the project.
3. If decisions regarding the proposed subdivision wiLL have
a material financial effect on your econornic interests, such an
effect wilI be dist ingui,shable from the effect on the public
genera 11y .
FACTS
You are a nember of the City of Rocklin Planning Conrnission.
You seek our advice to deternine whether you Inay participate in
pending governmental. decisions regarding a proposed subdivision
which is located near your residence in the city of Rocklin.
The project, known as The sunmit, is proposed for the top of
a hitl located to the east of your home within the city of
Rocklin. The developable portion of the project site is a 300 to
8oo foot wide hilltop rising 120 feet above the surrounding
neighborhoods, The hill runs essentially fron north to south.
There is existing residential development surroundi.ng the hill 
on
three sides; the west, the south, and the east.
The site is currentl.y zoned RE-z0. The development request
for a subdivision map and use perrnit is consistent with the
current zoning. The applicant is requesting a rezoning to PD-RE-
20 for technical. reasons. Thj.s rezoning will not change the
density allowed on the site. 
The RE-20 zone is a low density
residential zone, requiring minimum lots of 20, ooo square feet.
The city of Rocklin general plan contains a poli'cy
prohibiting developnent on hillsides with a greater than 20
percent slope. VirtualJ.y the entire west si.de of the hill has a
slope approximating 3o percent and is not proposed for
developnent. This slope is also heavily wooded. Access to the
hilltop, 
as $reLl as al.l utility 
hookups, are planned for the east
side of the hill. 
The reasons for this design include the
steepness of the slope and the number of trees on the west side
al,ong with the fact that the existing infrastructure on the east
side of the hill 
was designed to service the hilltop. 
A street in
an adjacent subdivision stubs into the logical access to the
hilltop and the utilities 
in the subdivisions adiacent to the site
on the east were designed to extend up to the hilJ-top. As a
result, you believe there will be no develoPment irnpact from the
project extending down the western slope.


our FiIe No. A-92-052
Page 3
i{hile not proposed, it is possible that a secondary access
could be designed that woul,d bring traffic 
down the northwest side
and along Clover valley Road. You state that even if this were
done, the increase in traffic 
on Clover Valley Road wouLd be
nominaL.
You ov/n a residence which is situated 230 feet fron the
bottom of the hil"Iside on the rrest. The entrance to the project
is rnore than one mile from your property. From your hone, you
cannot see the top of the ridge or any of the developable area.
The distance between your property line and the deveLopable
portion of the project is more than 3oo feet. 
Despite the
proximity of your lot to the project boundary, you believe that
your property is both physically and.visuaLly cut off from the
propoied- deve lopment . You have submitted for our consi-deration
rnaps showing the project site, the location of your home, and
other characteristics of the project.
You believe that the proposed developrnent wilL have no impact
on your residence because of the existing oak trees in.the open
=paie area, the slope of the hil1, and the fact that virtually aII
tiaffic 
and service! wilL access the site frour the east side of
the hil1. 
The city has retained a real estate appraiser who has
determined that the development would have no effect on the homes
in your ne ighborhood .
ANALYS I S
Section a71oo prchj.bits public officials 
:rom naking,
participating j.n, or using their official 
positron to influence a
iovernmlntal decision in which they know or have reason to know
ihey have a financial interest- 
As a planning comnissioner, you
are a pubJ,ic official. 
(section 82048')
An of f icial- has a financial interest in a deci-sion within the
meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable . that the
decisi6n wiII have a marerial f inancj.al effect, 
d istinguj.shable
from its effect on the public generally, on the of f j'cial, or on a
,.rl"i-.r 
ihe official's 
immediate fami1y,2 or on:
Any real property in which the public of f i.cial
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
2  An official′ s "immediate family“

covernnental Decis ion
A public official. nakes a governmentaf decision or
particilates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public officiat 
votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
6f actlon, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regul.ation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public
official 
participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authoiity of his or her position, the pub)-ic official:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive ieview, lrith a governnentaJ- entity or
private person regarding the decision,' or
(2) Advises or nakes recommendations to the
decis ion-rnaker, either directly or without
si.gnif icant interveni'ng substantive review, by3
(A) Conducting research or rnaking any
investigation $/hich requires the exercise of judg-
nent on the part of the official 
or designated
employee and the purpose of vhich is to influence
the decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, oraIIy or in writing, which
.eq,]-ir"= the exercise cf judgment on the part of
the official 
or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision'
Regulation 18700 (c) -
with regard to a governnental decj'sion which is within or
before an ofiicial's 
agency or an agency appointed by.or subiect
to the budgetary contr6l oi hj's or her agency, an official 
is
"it"*pti"g-to 
uie his or her official- position to influence the
decision if, ror the purpose of influencing the.decision,. the
official 
contacts, or-appears before, or otherwise attempts to
infLuence any member. oiiice., 
enPloyee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence inilude, but are not lirnited to'
uip"uiur,".= or contacts by the official 
on behalf of a business
..,irity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700' 1' )
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your
economic inteieit 
in your residence, which we presume to be worth
more than Sl,ooo, aifl be ;rateri'a1ly affected by decisions
regarding the proposed subdivision, you must not only di'squalify
i"iii"ir-r.o. 
p..ti"ipating 
in formar decisions of the pranning


our File No. A-92-052
Page 5
commission which may affect the value of your residence, but you
nust also abstain fron attempting to influence the decisions by
communicating with other members of the planning cornmission or the
staff regarding the decisions.
Foreseeabilitv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeabfe if there
is a substantial likel,ihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required. 
( Dor^rnev cares v.
Dor.rnev Communitv Development conrn. (1987) 196 CaI.App.3d 983, 989-
991; witt v. Morror, (L977) 7O Ca1.APp.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
l FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possib).e conflict of interest. 
(witt v. llorrow, supra at 823.)
You have stated j.n your request for advice that the wooded
hillside obscures the view of the site of the proposed subdivision
from your home. In addition, you state that the proposed
subdivisi.on will not result in an increase in traffic 
in your
neighborhood. However, it is foreseeable that a subdivision
locited at the top of a hill which is 230 feet from your horne will
have an effect on the value of your home. Even assuming that no
traffic will move through your neighborhood and that you will not
see this subdj-vision at the top of the hiII frorn your residence,
the proposed subdivj-sion may Iead to soil erosion which might wash
down thl west side of the hill 
where your home is located. It is
also foreseeable that there wiII be a change in the polLution and
noise levels j.n your neighborhood. Accordingly, the
foreseeability element is met. The next step is to deterrnine
whether the effect of the pending decisions on the value of your
residence wi 11 be material.
Mater ia I itv
ReguLatj.on 18702 sets forth the guidelines for deterrnining
ivhether an official's 
economic interest in a decision is
naterially affected by a decision. Your residence will be
affected lndirectly by the pending decis.ions. As you correctly
point out in your iequest for advice, when an of f ici'al's interests
in real propertjr are indirectlv involved in a governrnental
decision, the appropriate standard for deterrnining materiality is
that of Regulation 18702.3 which states in pertinent part that the
effect of i aecision is material as to real property in which an
official 
has a di.rect, indirect or benef icj-aI ownership interest
(not includj,ng a leasehold interest) , i'f the following applies:


Our File No. A-92-052
Page 6
The real property in which the official 
has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is
Iocated t ithin a 30o foot radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property lrhich
is the subject of the decision, unless the decision
vriLl have no financiaL effect upon the official's
real property interest.
Regulation :-8702.3(a) (1), emphasis added.
You have asked whether the slope area Located between your
hone and the deveLopable portion of the project site should be
counted as part of the developnent project for purposes of
applying Regulation L8702.3. Regulation LAl02.3 does not take
into consideration the contour of the land but rather Iimits
itself to setting standards for nateriality with respect to
dj.stances. Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3, the effect of
governnental decisions on real property situated within 3o0 feet
of property $rhich is the subject of a governnental decision is
presumed rnaterial. Therefore, unless you can shoi', that the
pending decisions wiIl have no financial effect on your residence,
your disqualification is required.
You have aLso asked r.rhat criteria or evidence should be used
to determine r.rhether or not a decision on a development project
erill have a financial effect on an official's 
ownershj-p interest
in real property. t'Ie have previously advised that a public
official 
is required to make a reasonable good faith effort to
determine the financial effects of a governmental decision on his
interests in real property. 
(Mcl,auqhlin Advice Letter, No. I-91-
141.) You have stlted that the city has sought the services of an
appraiser on your behalf and that this individual has deternined
tnit tne proposed subdivision wilI have no financial effect on
your resiience.3 Please note, however, that pursuant to
6ubdivision (d) of Regulation l-8702- 3, factors which must be
considered in determinj.ng whether a governmental decision wiIl
have a material. financial effect on an official's 
real property
include, but are not limited to:
(1) The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
the proposed project or change j.n use in
relationship to the property in r'rhich the official
has an interest;
(2) whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the deveLopment potential
or income producing potential of the property;
3  1n rendering advice′
 the
fact.  (In re Oqlesbv (■
975)
Comm■ss■on does not Act as a finder of
l FPPC Ops. 7■
′ 77.)


Our File No. A-92-052
Page 7
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not lirnited to, effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions. or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Regrulation 18702.3 (d) .
If it is determined that decisions relating to the Proposed
subdivision which is located within 3o0 feet of your residence
wiII have anv financial effect on the value of your residence,
your d isqual i f ication is requi.red unless the rrpublic generallyrl
exception applies, as discussed below.
PubIic cenera I lv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqua 1i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally'
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect aII members of
ine puutic in tire sane manner, disqualificatj-on nay be required
unlels the effect of the decision on the public official's
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our
diicussion, -.he public is the entire populatj.on of the city' 
(In
re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,' rn re owen (L9'76) 2 EPPC Ops. 77;
.Iorqensen Advice Letter, No. i-90-017.)
Your home is located within 23o feet of the hill on which the
proposed subdivision will be located. The proiect wiLl tsower 120
tee-t above your neighborhood. Under thj's set of facts, it is
apparent tnit decisions regarding the proposed subdivision will
a?iect your economic interests and those of your inrnediate -
neighbois in a manner whi.ch is di.stingu j.shable from the effect on
individuals who are more renoved from the project. 
Accordingly,
the "public generally" exception is not applicable to your facts.
Bifurcation
Under certain cj,rcumstances, large and conplex decisions may
be divided into separate decisions '.rhen an official 
has a
disqualifying interest in one component of ihe decisions which i.s
not interdependent upon other components. Ilowever, under some
circunstances, a serles of decisions may be '!oo interrelated to be
considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119')
This would be the case if a decision on one aspect of the


our File No. A-92-052
Page 8
subdivision wiIl substantially affect a decision on another aspect
of the subdivision. Under such circumstances, if you must
disqualify yourself from participating in one of the decisions,
you are a).so disqualified from participating in the other
decisions.
For exanple, in a decision to seLect one of two autopark
sites, a decision to select one of the sites is essentially a
decision against the other autopark site' 
(Booqaard Advice
Letter, No. I-90-347.) Sinilarly, decisions regarding the
proposed subdivision nay be so interrelated that decisions
regarding one aspect of the subdivision wilI affect other
deiisions regarding the proposed subdivision.
However, if it is possible to consider one particular aspect
of the proposed subdivision independently of the others, and if
the items are not interrelated, theY may be bifurcated. If
certain decisions regarding the proposed subdj.vision may be
bifurcated, then you would be required to abstain from
participating in discussions of disqualifying aspects of the
decisions while participatj.ng freely in other decisions regarding
the subdivision.
For example, you state in your request for advice that. while
not proposed, it is possible that a secondary access could be
aesignei for the proposed subdivision which would bring traffic
down the northwest s j.de of the hill and along clover valley Road.
If this road is built, j.t w j'11 result in an increase in traffic 
in
your neighborhood which wilL foreseeabj-y affect the quality of
life in your neighborhood and potentj.ally have an effect on your
economic interesi in your residence. conversely, decisions
concerning the tyPe of pavement to be used in the proPosed
subdivision are li.kely to have no effect at aII on the value of
your residence.
If you determine that certain decisions regarding the
proposed subdivision are not so interrelated that decisions
iegirding one aspect of the subdivision wiIl affect other
aecisioni, such decisions may be bifurcated and considered
separately from other decisions regarding the subdivision. In
orier to Lifr.,.cat" the pending decisions, the planning commission
must adhere to the following procedure:
(i) 
The decisions in wh j.ch you have a dj'squalLfying
irnancial interest must be segregated from other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which you are disqualified nust be
ccnsj,dered first, 
and a final decision must be reached by the
planning commissj-on without your Participation.
(3) once a final decision has been reached on a particular
item, you may partj.cipate in the deLiberations and vote regarding


Our Fi■e No. A-92-052
Page 9
other matterS in the proposed subdivision so 
■ong as those
de■iberations and VOte dO not affect the prev■
ous dec■s■ons from
which you were disqua■
ified。  (Merkuloff Advice Letter′
 No. ■-90-
542, McNatt AdViCe Letter′

■-90-7■4.)
We trust this 
■etter adequate■y responds to your inquiry.

sections.question:
1. What criteria or evidence should a public official use to determine whether or not a decision on a development project will have a financial effect on that official's ownership interest in real property?
2. Should the slope area located between your home and the developable portion of the project site be counted as part of the development project for purposes of applying Regulation 18702.3?
3. If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the proposed subdivision will have a material financial effect on the value of your residence which is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
sections.conclusion:
1. A public official is required to make a reasonable good faith effort to determine the financial effects of a governmental decision on his economic interests.
2. Regulation 18702.3 does not take into consideration the contour of the land. It only sets forth standards for determining materiality in terms of distance to the boundaries of the project.
3. If decisions regarding the proposed subdivision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests, such an effect will be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

sections.question_synthetic: A planning commissioner seeks advice on whether he must disqualify himself from voting on a proposed subdivision near his residence, specifically regarding the criteria for determining financial effects on real property and the application of materiality standards under Regulation 18702.3.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the official must make a good faith effort to determine financial effects, that materiality is measured by distance (not topography) under Regulation 18702.3, and that the proposed subdivision's effects on the official's residence would likely be material and distinguishable from effects on the public generally, requiring disqualification.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains all standard sections (Questions, Conclusions, Facts, Analysis). OCR quality is moderate with some character errors (e.g., 'cornmission' instead of 'commission'). Text appears truncated at end of last visible page. All major substantive content extracted successfully.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. What criteria or evidence should a public official use to determine whether or not a decision on a development project will have a financial effect on that official's ownership interest in real property?
2. Should the slope area located between your home and the developable portion of the project site be counted as part of the development project for purposes of applying Regulation 18702.3?
3. If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the proposed subdivision will have a material financial effect on the value of your residence which is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?

CONCLUSION: 1. A public official is required to make a reasonable good faith effort to determine the financial effects of a governmental decision on his economic interests.
2. Regulation 18702.3 does not take into consideration the contour of the land. It only sets forth standards for determining materiality in terms of distance to the boundaries of the project.
3. If decisions regarding the proposed subdivision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests, such an effect will be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

If decisions regarding the proposed subdivision wiLL have a material financial effect on your econornic interests, such an effect wilI be dist ingui,shable from the effect on the public genera 11y . FACTS You are a nember of the City of Rocklin Planning Conrnission. You seek our advice to deternine whether you Inay participate in pending governmental. decisions regarding a proposed subdivision which is located near your residence in the city of Rocklin. The project, known as The sunmit, is proposed for the top of a hitl located to the east of your home within the city of Rocklin. The developable portion of the project site is a 300 to 8oo foot wide hilltop rising 120 feet above the surrounding neighborhoods, The hill runs essentially fron north to south. There is existing residential development surroundi.ng the hill on three sides; the west, the south, and the east. The site is currentl.y zoned RE-z0. The development request for a subdivision map and use perrnit is consistent with the current zoning. The applicant is requesting a rezoning to PD-RE- 20 for technical. reasons. Thj.s rezoning will not change the density allowed on the site. The RE-20 zone is a low density residential zone, requiring minimum
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-92-052 addresses a Rocklin Planning Commissioner's request regarding potential conflicts of interest in decisions affecting a proposed hillside subdivision near his residence, concluding that the official must disqualify himself if material financial effects on his property are reasonably foreseeable, based on distance standards rather than topographical factors.
